verses.text_saheeh_international: And they found a servant from among Our servants to whom we had given mercy from us and had taught him from Us a [certain] knowledge.
verses.arabic_text: فَوَجَدَا عَبْدًا مِّنْ عِبَادِنَآ ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّا عِلْمًا

verses.text_saheeh_international: Moses said to him, "May I follow you on [the condition] that you teach me from what you have been taught of sound judgment?"
verses.arabic_text: قَالَ لَهُۥ مُوسَىٰ هَلْ أَتَّبِعُكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُعَلِّمَنِ مِمَّا عُلِّمْتَ رُشْدًا

verses.text_saheeh_international: He said, "Indeed, you will never be able to have patience with me.
verses.arabic_text: قَالَ إِنَّكَ لَن تَسْتَطِيعَ مَعِىَ صَبْرًا

verses.text_saheeh_international: And how can you have patience for that which you have not encompassed in knowledge?"
verses.arabic_text: وَكَيْفَ تَصْبِرُ عَلَىٰ مَا لَمْ تُحِطْ بِهِۦ خُبْرًا

verses.text_saheeh_international: Moses said, "You will find me, if Allah wills, patient, and I will not disobey you in [any] order."
verses.arabic_text: قَالَ سَتَجِدُنِىٓ إِن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ صَابِرًا وَلَآ أَعْصِى لَكَ أَمْرًا

verses.text_saheeh_international: He said, "Then if you follow me, do not ask me about anything until I make mention of it to you."
verses.arabic_text: قَالَ فَإِنِ ٱتَّبَعْتَنِى فَلَا تَسْـَٔلْنِى عَن شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰٓ أُحْدِثَ لَكَ مِنْهُ ذِكْرًا

tafsir_explanations.explanation: Al-Qurtubi's tafsir is not available for this verse.

**The Meeting with Al-Khidr (Verse 65)**

This verse marks the culmination of Prophet Musa's journey to seek knowledge from a special servant of Allah. The narrative begins with Musa's determination to reach the junction of the two seas, a place indicated by Allah as the location of this knowledgeable individual. The sign for reaching this spot was the disappearance of a salted fish they carried, which miraculously came back to life and swam into the sea, leaving a distinct path.

**Identity of the Servant and Nature of Knowledge**

Ibn Kathir clarifies that the

mentioned in the verse is Al-Khidr, peace be upon him. This is confirmed by authentic Hadith from the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Al-Khidr was granted a unique form of knowledge directly from Allah, described as

This knowledge differed from the prophetic knowledge given to Musa, highlighting that Allah bestows various types of wisdom upon His chosen servants.

**The Hadith of Musa and Al-Khidr**

According to a Hadith narrated by Ubayy bin Ka'b and recorded by Al-Bukhari, the journey began after Musa, in a speech to the Children of Israel, stated he was the most learned. Allah then revealed to him that there was a servant at the junction of the two seas who was more knowledgeable. Musa embarked on this journey with his boy-servant, Yusha' bin Nun. The fish, revived by the 'Ayn Al-Ḥayāt (Spring of Life), escaped into the sea, creating a tunnel-like path. Yusha' forgot to inform Musa immediately, and they continued traveling until Musa felt exhaustion. Upon being reminded of the fish's miraculous return to life, Musa realized they had passed their destination and retraced their steps to find Al-Khidr.

**Musa's Request and Al-Khidr's Warning (Verses 66-68)**

Upon finding Al-Khidr, Musa respectfully asked,
This demonstrates the humility required in seeking knowledge from a scholar. Al-Khidr immediately warned Musa, stating,

Al-Khidr understood that his actions would appear contrary to Musa's understanding of divine law and justice, as Al-Khidr operated based on a deeper, divinely revealed wisdom that Musa did not yet possess.

**Musa's Promise and Al-Khidr's Condition (Verses 69-70)**

Musa, eager to learn, promised patience, saying,
acknowledging that true patience and obedience are only possible with divine aid. Al-Khidr then laid down a crucial condition for Musa's companionship:
This condition was essential for the journey to proceed, requiring Musa to suspend his immediate judgment and trust in Al-Khidr's divinely guided actions.

cross_references.relevance: Ibn Kathir references this verse (Out of them both come out pearl and coral) to illustrate how a statement can attribute an action to two things, even if only one is directly responsible, similar to how forgetfulness was attributed to both Musa and Yusha' even though Yusha' was the one who forgot the fish.

lessons_practical_applications.point: Humility in seeking knowledge: Even a great prophet like Musa sought knowledge from another servant of Allah, demonstrating that one should always remain humble and open to learning, regardless of one's own status or existing knowledge.

The diversity of divine knowledge: Allah grants different types of knowledge to different individuals. Not all wisdom is accessible through conventional means or even prophetic revelation, and some knowledge is hidden or specific to certain individuals.

Patience and trust in divine wisdom: The story emphasizes the importance of patience and trusting in a higher wisdom, especially when faced with events that seem contradictory or unjust from a limited human perspective. This applies to trusting Allah's decrees even when their reasons are not immediately apparent.

The importance of fulfilling promises and conditions: Musa's promise of patience and Al-Khidr's condition not to question highlight the sanctity of agreements and the need for discipline in the pursuit of knowledge and spiritual growth.

summary: These verses narrate the beginning of Prophet Musa's extraordinary journey to seek a special form of knowledge from Al-Khidr, a servant of Allah endowed with unique divine wisdom. The account details their meeting, Al-Khidr's warning about Musa's inability to be patient with his actions, and Musa's promise of patience under the condition that he would not question anything until Al-Khidr himself explained it. This narrative underscores the profound lessons of humility, the diverse nature of divine knowledge, and the necessity of patience and trust in Allah's wisdom.
